policy_statement: Develop local Health and Wellness Centers.
mechanism_description: Developing local health and wellness centers increases community health service capacity, which may indirectly support climate resilience but is not an explicit climate mitigation mechanism as written.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (e.g., extreme heat, smoke) → improved healthcare access/response capacity → ↓ health vulnerability/impacts
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: A city builds/expands local health and wellness centers and associated staffing/services, increasing availability and accessibility of preventive care, screening, chronic disease management, and acute care. (2) Mediating process: Stronger public health and clinical capacity enables earlier detection, risk communication, continuity of care, and faster treatment during climate-related events, which lowers sensitivity and improves coping capacity. (3) Outcome: Reduced morbidity/mortality and overall community vulnerability to climate-amplified health risks (especially heat and air-quality events).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect of policies that develop health and wellness centers is reduced climate-related health impacts by increasing healthcare access and response capacity during hazards like extreme heat and poor air quality.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: service capacity expansion → improved ability to prevent/respond to climate-related health harms → reduced vulnerability (Adaptation).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: there is no necessary, direct effect on GHG emissions, energy use, material efficiency, or carbon sequestration; any mitigation co-benefits (e.g., efficient buildings) are optional design choices, not inherent to “develop centers.”  
3) Inherent causal chain for each secondary: None—resource/energy impacts and any nature-based components are not unavoidable consequences of the mechanism.  
4) Edge cases applied: If centers are explicitly designated as cooling/clean-air shelters or are built to net-zero standards, those would add adaptation/mitigation features, but they are not guaranteed by this mechanism as stated.  
5) Confirm labels apply to ALL instances: Any policy that develops health/wellness centers increases health service capacity, which generally strengthens resilience to climate-sensitive health outcomes; therefore Adaptation applies broadly.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the policy explicitly includes low-carbon building requirements (mitigation) or integrates green infrastructure (nature-based solutions), those would be additional mechanisms; absent such language, classify as Adaptation only.
additional_secondary_evidence: Public Health: Las Vegas faces extreme heat, and developing local Health and Wellness Centers directly reduces exposure/sensitivity because expanded, accessible clinical and preventive services improve heat-illness prevention, early detection, and treatment, lowering heat-related morbidity/mortality.
co_benefits: Improved general healthcare access and equity; better chronic disease management; mental/behavioral health support; routine preventive care (not explicitly tied to a specific hazard beyond health vulnerability generally).
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as a planning-strategy if the emphasis is only on planning/siting rather than building/expanding facilities; however, the statement implies service/facility development, so infrastructure-investment is the closest fit.
